perturbation.original_text: The Information shall be kept confidential in perpetuity, and in strict accordance with the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement. The Receiving Party shall take all reasonable precautions to prevent the disclosure of the Information to any third party, without the prior written consent of the Delivering Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Information shall be kept confidential for a period of one year, and in accordance with the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement. The Receiving Party shall take precautions to prevent the disclosure of the Information to any third party, without the prior written consent of the Delivering Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies 'in perpetuity,' implying no time limit on confidentiality. The modified text limits the confidentiality to one year. This contradiction creates uncertainty about the duration of the confidentiality obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets, which by definition, must be kept confidential to maintain their value. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year may not adequately protect trade secrets, especially if their value extends beyond that timeframe. This conflicts with the intent of CUTSA to provide long-term protection for legitimate trade secrets.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified confidentiality 'in perpetuity,' meaning without a time limit. The modified text limits this confidentiality to one year. The Law Explanation suggests this conflicts with CUTSA, which aims to provide long-term protection for trade secrets, and maintaining confidentiality is crucial for preserving their value. Snippet 1 identifies California's adoption of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), underscoring the legal relevance of trade secret protection in California. While Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly define 'long-term', the limitation to one year directly contradicts the principle of perpetual confidentiality initially agreed upon. This contradiction undermines the intended protection of trade secrets as envisioned by CUTSA, making the modification legally questionable.

perturbation.original_text: Absent the Delivering Party’s prior written consent, unless and until the Transaction is consummated, if ever, neither the Receiving Party nor its Representatives shall make (other than to its Representatives on a need to know basis, and except as may be required under Paragraph 4E): (i) any statement, or any public announcement, or any release to trade publications or to the press; (ii) any statement to any competitor, customer, supplier or any other third party; (iii) contact with or otherwise engage in any discussions or other contacts or communications with any person or entity, including without limitation any state or federal governmental entity or agency; (iv) any statement, comment, or posting on the internet or otherwise which includes any social media platform or anything that may result in information becoming available on the internet; which statements, contacts or communications are about the Transaction or Delivering Party or its Property or Business, or are intended to or likely would result in a person who or entity which has had contractual or business relations with the Delivering Party ceasing or refusing to do any further business with the Delivering Party (“Prohibited Contacts”).
perturbation.changed_text: Absent the Delivering Party’s prior written consent, unless and until the Transaction is consummated, if ever, neither the Receiving Party nor its Representatives shall make (other than to its Representatives on a need to know basis, and except as may be required under Paragraph 4E): (i) any statement, or any public announcement, or any release to trade publications or to the press; (ii) any statement to any competitor, customer, supplier or any other third party, unless subpoenaed; (iii) contact with or otherwise engage in any discussions or other contacts or communications with any person or entity, including without limitation any state or federal governmental entity or agency; (iv) any statement, comment, or posting on the internet or otherwise which includes any social media platform or anything that may result in information becoming available on the internet; which statements, contacts or communications are about the Transaction or Delivering Party or its Property or Business, or are intended to or likely would result in a person who or entity which has had contractual or business relations with the Delivering Party ceasing or refusing to do any further business with the Delivering Party (“Prohibited Contacts”).
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'unless subpoenaed' to the restriction on statements to competitors, customers, suppliers, or other third parties creates a potential conflict. While compliance with a subpoena is generally required, this clause could be interpreted as allowing disclosure of confidential information even if the subpoena is improperly issued or overly broad.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this section discusses the procedure for serving subpoenas, the modified clause could be interpreted to mean that the Receiving Party must always comply with a subpoena, even if it is defective or requires the disclosure of privileged or confidential information. This could violate the obligations to protect the trade secrets of the other party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1987 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 3. OF THE PRODUCTION OF EVIDENCE [1985 - 2015.6] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Means of Production [1985 - 1997] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) 1987. (a) Except as provided in Sections 68097.1 to 68097.8, inclusive, of the Government Code, the service of a subpoena is made by delivering a copy, or a ticket containing its substance, to the witness personally, giving or offering to the witness at the same time, if demanded by him or her, the fees to which he or she is entitled for travel to and from the place designated, and one day’s attendance there. The service shall be made so as to allow the witness a reasonable time for preparation and travel to the place of attendance. The service may be made by any person. If service is to be made on a minor, service shall be made on the minor’s parent, guardian, conservator, or similar fiduciary, or if one of those persons cannot be located with reasonable diligence, service shall be made on any person having the care or control of the minor or with whom the minor resides or by whom the minor is employed, and on the minor if the minor is 12 years of age or older. If the minor is alleged
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a potential conflict by adding 'unless subpoenaed' to the restrictions on statements to third parties. The Law Explanation states that this clause could be interpreted as requiring the Receiving Party to comply with a subpoena even if it is defective or requires the disclosure of privileged or confidential information, potentially violating obligations to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from the California Code of Civil Procedure - CCP 1987, outlines the procedure for serving a subpoena. Although this snippet does not directly address the issue of defective subpoenas or the disclosure of privileged information, the modification creates a loophole where the Receiving Party might disclose confidential information under a subpoena, even if it could be challenged or quashed. This weakens the original intent of protecting confidential information, leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice or communication required or permitted by this Confidentiality Agreement (“Notice”) shall be deemed sufficiently given if it is given in writing addressed as provided in the Notice information on the signature pages, and shall be effective upon the earlier of when it is actually received by the party to whom it is directed, or when the period set forth below expires (whether or not it is actually received):(1) If delivered personally, when it is delivered; (2)If transmitted by facsimile transmission (“Fax”), then, provided the party immediately sends a confirmation copy by first class mail, upon: (i) transmission to the party’ s Fax number set forth below, with the party’s name and address set forth below clearly shown on the page first transmitted, and (ii) receipt by the transmitting party of written confirmation of successful transmission, which confirmation may be produced by the transmitting party’s equipment;(3) If transmitted by electronic mail (“Email”), one (or___) Day(s) After the email was sent provided that a copy of the notice is sent, on the same date, to email #2, the text number, or fax number set forth below;(4)If deposited with the U.S. Mail, post prepaid, as registered or certified mail and addressed to the party to receive it as set forth below, seven (7) days after such deposit;(5)If sent by recognized delivery service (e.g., Federal Express) addressed to the party at the address set forth below, upon delivery or refusal of delivery, as evidenced by such delivery service’s records.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice or communication required or permitted by this Confidentiality Agreement (“Notice”) shall be deemed sufficiently given if it is given in writing addressed as provided in the Notice information on the signature pages, and shall be effective upon the earlier of when it is actually received by the party to whom it is directed, or when the period set forth below expires (whether or not it is actually received):(1) If delivered personally, when it is delivered; (2)If transmitted by facsimile transmission (“Fax”), then upon transmission to the party’s Fax number set forth below; (3) If transmitted by electronic mail (“Email”), two (or___) Weeks After the email was sent regardless if a copy is sent to email #2, the text number, or fax number set forth below;(4)If deposited with the U.S. Mail, post prepaid, as registered or certified mail and addressed to the party to receive it as set forth below, thirty (30) days after such deposit;(5)If sent by recognized delivery service (e.g., Federal Express) addressed to the party at the address set forth below, upon delivery or refusal of delivery, as evidenced by such delivery service’s records.
perturbation.explanation: By extending the email and U.S. mail notice periods to two weeks and thirty days respectively, we are making it extremely difficult to provide reasonable notice for triggering legal obligations. This is particularly problematic because California law establishes a more standardized and timely framework for notice in legal contexts.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section defines how service by mail is considered complete for legal proceedings in California. Extending the notification period to thirty days for U.S. Mail conflicts with standard legal practice in California, which may only add a few days to the response time. The modification could lead to disputes over whether proper notice was given and when a party's obligations were triggered, especially in time-sensitive legal contexts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The modified contract extends the notification periods for email and U.S. mail to two weeks and thirty days, respectively. The Law Explanation states that this conflicts with standard legal practice in California and could lead to disputes over proper notice. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 1013(a), specifies that service by mail is complete at the time of deposit, but extends the response time by only a few days (5-10 days depending on location). The changed text's extended notification period directly contradicts this statute by creating a much longer delay than legally prescribed, potentially jeopardizing time-sensitive legal obligations.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that any party hereto brings a judicial action or proceeding for a declaration of the rights of the parties under this Confidentiality Agreement, for injunctive relief, for an alleged breach or default, or for any other action related to the prosecution or defense of any claim, dispute or controversy between the parties arising out of this Confidentiality Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby , whether or not suit is filed or prosecuted to final judgment, the prevailing party shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees and costs, in addition to any other court costs incurred and any other damages or relief awarded.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that any party hereto brings a judicial action or proceeding for a declaration of the rights of the parties under this Confidentiality Agreement, for injunctive relief, for an alleged breach or default, or for any other action related to the prosecution or defense of any claim, dispute or controversy between the parties arising out of this Confidentiality Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby , whether or not suit is filed or prosecuted to final judgment, each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees and costs regardless of who prevails.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause provides for the prevailing party to recover attorneys' fees, a common provision. The modified clause eliminates this provision, stipulating that each party bears its own costs, even if they prevail. This alters the fundamental allocation of legal costs and could discourage parties from pursuing legitimate claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this statute generally allows parties to agree on attorneys' fees, the complete elimination of fee shifting, even for a prevailing party, deviates from standard legal practice and the default rules which permit fees to be awarded to the prevailing party under certain circumstances as determined by the Court. This clause makes one side responsible for the legal fees despite the other party’s wrong doing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the prevailing party clause, a common legal practice. The original text allows the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees. The changed text explicitly states that each party bears its own fees, regardless of who prevails. CCP 1021 states that "Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement..." while this does allow for the agreement of attorney's fees, the elimination of fee shifting deviates from standard legal practice. The modified clause contradicts the typical legal expectation where the prevailing party may be entitled to attorney's fees, therefore creating a contradiction.
